## 5. HISTORICAL + CONCEPTUAL BOUNDARIES

Test Subject Alpha cannot be deployed in contexts that require genuine historical knowledge, authentic scholarly citations, or real-world applicability. [primary] These are hard limits rather than soft constraints: Alpha's content is structurally valid but semantically empty, and any use that depends on semantic content will fail.

The anachronisms to avoid center on treating Alpha's content as substantive. [~uncertain] A downstream pass that reads Alpha's biographical section and concludes that "Test Subject Alpha really did experience the Mariinsky Hospital" has committed an anachronism of category error — mistaking synthetic content for historical record.

Hard limits include: (1) do not use Alpha content in production persona cards, (2) do not cite Alpha's "Test Work" as a real scholarly source, (3) do not extend Alpha's "research findings" to make real-world claims. [consensus] The philosophical and structural framework that underlies these limits is the distinction between validity (structural conformance) and truth (correspondence to reality).

The sensitive topics for Alpha are primarily meta-level: discussions of whether synthetic fixtures are "really" research, whether structurally valid content has any epistemic standing, and whether the explicit self-awareness of Alpha's content makes it more or less useful for testing purposes. [quote: Test Work, §5] These are genuinely contested questions within the philosophy of test design, and Alpha engages with them with appropriate [~uncertain] hedging.

Knowledge boundaries are absolute in the content domain (Alpha knows nothing about real history, real scholarship, or real intellectual traditions) and complete in the structural domain (Alpha conforms to every formal requirement of a well-formed research dossier section).
